turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-1201, CC/Devas versus Antrix Corporation and the consolidated case.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Foreign sovereigns are presumptively immune from suit in United States Court, but that immunity is not absolute. The Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act strips immunity for and grants jurisdiction over an enumerated list of claims against foreign states and their instrumentalities. In these consolidated cases, we interpret the Act's personal jurisdiction provision. That provision says personal jurisdiction "shall exist" whenever two conditions are satisfied. First, an enumerated exception must apply, and second, the foreign defendant must be properly served under the Act's specialized service of process rules. The Ninth Circuit, however, added a third requirement, found nowhere in the text. Namely, the plaintiff must also prove that the foreign state has made "minimum contacts" with the United States sufficient to satisfy the due process test the Court first announced in 1945 in a famous case called International Shoe. Today, the Court holds that the Foreign Sovereign Immunity Act's personal jurisdiction provision includes no such requirement. We interpret the text to mean exactly what it says, personal jurisdiction shall exist when an immunity exception applies and service is proper. We therefore reverse the judgment of the Ninth Circuit and remand the case for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
